title: SEGMENT 17 — What HGP did NOT solve: polygenic disease, regulatory variants, missing heritability

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — What HGP did NOT solve: polygenic disease, regulatory variants, missing heritability
This is the honest segment.
I have been mostly celebrating HGP for nine segments.
Now I have to be candid about what HGP promised, what it did not deliver, and what we are still struggling with.
The 1990s rhetoric around HGP, including some of Collins's own speeches and a great deal of media coverage, suggested that sequencing the genome would crack the genetic basis of common diseases.
Heart disease, diabetes, schizophrenia, autism, Alzheimer's — these would all yield their genetic secrets once we had the sequence.
Personalized medicine, in which your treatment is tailored to your genome, would arrive within a decade or two.
Twenty-three years after the 2003 finished sequence, that vision has been only partially realized.
Here is the honest accounting.
For Mendelian disorders — diseases caused by single-gene mutations with simple inheritance patterns — HGP has been transformative.

Diseases like cystic fibrosis, sickle cell anemia, Huntington's disease, the rare childhood metabolic disorders, the rare immunodeficiencies — these are diagnosed routinely now through clinical sequencing.

The rare disease diagnostic odyssey, which used to take families an average of five to seven years and dozens of specialists, can now often be resolved in weeks with whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing.
Thousands of new disease genes have been identified, and an increasing number of rare diseases have targeted treatments.
For common complex diseases — heart disease, type 2 diabetes, schizophrenia, depression, most cancers, autoimmune diseases — the story is far more humbling.

When genome-wide association studies, GWAS, became feasible around 2005 to 2007, the field expected to find a small number of common variants of large effect that would explain most of the genetic risk for common diseases.
The first GWAS results, around 2007, found something very different.

What GWAS found, repeatedly, across disease after disease, was that the genetic architecture of common disease is polygenic — meaning that risk is influenced by hundreds or thousands of common variants, each of small individual effect, scattered across the genome.
Schizophrenia: more than three hundred associated loci, each contributing a tiny fraction of risk.
Height — a non-disease trait studied as a model — more than twelve thousand associated variants, together explaining maybe forty percent of the heritability.
Diabetes, hypertension, obesity, depression: all polygenic, all with hundreds of loci, all with the same picture.
This was not what people expected, and it was not what HGP rhetoric had implicitly promised.
The world of single-gene tests for common diseases largely did not materialize, because there are no single genes to test.
The second uncomfortable finding from GWAS is that the vast majority of associated variants — somewhere between eighty-five and ninety percent — are not in protein-coding sequence.
They are in regulatory regions, in introns, in intergenic space.

These are variants that presumably affect when and how much specific genes are expressed in specific tissues, but tracing the path from a non-coding variant to a specific functional consequence is enormously harder than tracing a coding variant that changes a specific amino acid in a specific protein.
The third uncomfortable finding is what is called missing heritability.
For many traits, twin studies suggest that genetic factors explain, say, seventy or eighty percent of variation in the population — meaning the trait is highly heritable.
But the GWAS-identified variants together explain only ten or twenty or thirty percent.
Where is the missing heritability?

The answer is some combination of: rare variants not captured by SNP arrays, structural variants not captured by short-read sequencing, gene-by-gene interactions not captured by single-variant association tests, gene-by-environment interactions not captured at all, and overestimates of heritability from twin studies due to shared environment confounding.
Some of the missing heritability is being recovered as biobank-scale sequencing reveals rare variants.
Some of it may never be recoverable in the form that twin studies suggested it exists.
The honest summary is this.
HGP gave us the reference.
The reference enabled GWAS.
GWAS revealed that common disease is polygenic, regulatory, and genuinely complex.
That is not what was promised in the 1990s, but it is what is true.

Personalized medicine has arrived in some specific high-leverage areas — pharmacogenomics, oncology, rare disease — but the broader vision of "your genome tells your doctor exactly what will happen to you and what to do about it" has not arrived and may never arrive in that form.
The genome is one input.
Environment, behavior, age, and stochastic biological events are also inputs, and they cannot be inferred from the sequence alone.
This is not a failure of HGP.
It is a correction of an overpromise.
The science is harder than the rhetoric suggested.
The Human Genome Project did exactly what it set out to do — produce the reference sequence.
It is everything we have learned from that reference that has shown us how complicated the path from sequence to disease actually is.
